title: Micromorphic effects in an octet truss lattice
abstract: Elastic wave dispersion is studied in an octet truss lattice and compared with a designed rib lattice known to exhibit strong Cosserat elastic effects. Dispersion entails variation of wave speed with frequency. The phenomenon is experimentally investigated by exciting standing waves in specimens of different length at discrete frequencies. At lower frequencies corresponding to long wavelengths, wave propagation is classically non-dispersive. As wavelength approaches a small multiple of the rib length, dispersion is observed. The material exhibited cut-off frequencies above which no signals were propagated. The physical origin of the dispersion and cut-off is resonance of the ribs. Interpreted as micromorphic continua, cellular solid behavior reveals elastic constants associated with flexibility of the unit cell in comparison with that of the overall material.
summary: This paper explores how elastic waves behave in lattice structures like the octet truss. It finds that at high frequencies, the wave speed changes (dispersion) and eventually stops propagating entirely (cut-off) due to the resonance of the lattice ribs. The study proves that a 'micromorphic' continuum theory is necessary to accurately model these effects, as simpler models like Cosserat elasticity cannot account for the observed dispersion. This provides a more complete mathematical framework for designing advanced metamaterials.
main_problem: The study investigates the physical origin of elastic wave dispersion and cut-off frequencies in octet truss lattices and seeks a continuum-level theoretical representation for these phenomena.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that the micromorphic elasticity theory successfully accounts for both longitudinal wave dispersion and cut-off frequencies, whereas Cosserat elasticity fails to predict the dispersion. They attribute the observed wave phenomena to the resonance of the lattice ribs.
method: Experimental investigation via standing wave excitation, Resonant Ultrasound Spectroscopy (RUS), and wave transmission tests, compared against analytical predictions using micromorphic and Cosserat continuum models.
model_or_system: The physical systems are Ti-5553 titanium alloy and polyamide polymer octet truss lattices. The theoretical models include classical elasticity, Cosserat (micropolar) elasticity, and micromorphic (microstructure) elasticity.
key_observables: Phase velocity (Vph), group velocity (Vg), cut-off frequencies (fc), and the normalized micromorphic effect (Lambda).
important_parameters: Angular frequency (omega), wave number (k), cell size, rib length, rib thickness, and the dimensionless parameter Lambda.
main_assumptions: The calculation of cut-off frequencies assumes the ribs behave as simply supported bars; the analysis of the polymer lattice is noted as being complicated by potential viscoelasticity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the relationship between frequency and wavenumber for phase and group velocity; Figure 2 plots dispersion for the titanium lattice; Figure 3 plots dispersion for the polymer lattice; Figure 4 illustrates the micro-deformation gradient and double stress in micromorphic theory.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of wave dispersion in lattices, describes the experimental setup and materials, presents the observed dispersion and cut-off data, compares different continuum models (Classical, Cosserat, Micromorphic), and concludes with a theoretical interpretation of the results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A representative graph of angular frequency ω = 2πf versus wave number k = 2π

Figure 2: Elastic wave dispersion, frequency f vs. inverse length 1/L, in a Ti-5553 titanium alloy octet lattice. The specimen length is L = λ/2. Inset: image of lattice structure; the cell size is 4.5 mm.

Figure 3: Elastic wave dispersion, frequency f vs. inverse length, in a polymer lattice with designed hollow ribs. The specimen length is L = λ/2. Inset: image of lattice structure; the cell size is 9 mm in the longitudinal direction. Adapted from [9].

Figure 4: Gradients of micro-deformation κpqr and corresponding double stress µpqr, adapted from [4]. Arrows represent forces. Top: Cosserat freedom within micromorphic framework. Bottom: micromorphic freedom for axial compression.
